title: Question 2
stem: A 56 year old woman presents with bilateral eyelid lesions. Histopathology is shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
teaching_point: Xanthelasma is correct because there are abundant foamy histiocytes in the superficial dermis. chalazion typically shows mixed acute and chronic inflammation with extracellular lipid deposits; it is sometimes granulomatous. Erdheim-Chester is a non-Langerhans histiocytosis with intermixed lymphocytes, which may involve the orbit but also has involvement of other organ systems: bone, CNS, pituitary. there is not an abundant mixed inflammatory infiltrate with histiocytes showing grooved nuclei. ruptured epidermal inclusion cyst would show portions of epithelial lines cyst with a surrounding mixed inflammatory infiltrate, often with keratin debris and foreign body giant cells, many of which may contain keratin fragments. Xanthelasma

title: Question 3
stem: A 50 year old woman presents with bilateral medial upper eyelid yellow plaques. Which systemic medical condition is most likely associated with this pathology?
teaching_point: Atherosclerosis. Xanthelasma characteristically presents with symmetric medial upper eyelid yellow plaques. It may be associated with cirrhosis, atherosclerosis, thyroid disorders, nephrotic syndrome, primary hyperlipidemias (especially type 2a) and diabetes mellitus. acromegaly is growth hormone overproduction and is not associated with xanthelasma. breast cancer is not associated with xanthelasma. Metastatic breast cancer can cause enophthalmos but not yellow plaques. colon cancer is not associated with xanthelasma. Xanthelasma

title: Question 4
stem: Among melanocytic lesions of the conjunctiva, which of the following statements is true about the lesion shown in the above image?
teaching_point: . It is the most common melanocytic lesion of the conjunctiva. This is a conjunctival compound nevus, which is the most common melanocytic lesion of the conjunctiva. They are benign. Differentials include primary acquired melanosis, racial melanosis, ocular melanocytosis and malignant melanoma. conjunctival nevi have a low rate of conversion to conjunctival melanoma (less than 1%). enucleation is a drastic treatment for a benign lesion. These are typically managed by periodic observation and serial photographic comparison. Removal is typically for cosmetic reasons or if there are concerning changes in color, shape or size. the morphology is usually a well defined, slightly elevated mass. The classic location is the interpalpebral bulbar conjunctiva, caruncle or plica semilunaris. , most often, these lesions are apparent within the first or second decade of life as a discrete, pigmented lesion. WHO classification of tumors of the conjunctiva and caruncle

title: Question 5
stem: In addition to special staining to evaluate elastic fibers, which of the following antibody panels is most useful for evaluating temporal arteritis?
teaching_point: CD3 / CD68. The hallmark of temporal arteritis is a transmural lymphocytic (T cells) and macrophagic infiltrate, which can be demonstrated with antibodies against CD3 and CD68, respectively. B cells (CD20+) and plasma cells (CD138+) are not diagnostic findings. the combination of CD34 and CD68 highlights histiocytes, myofibroblasts and small blood vessels but excludes T cells. although CD4+ and CD8+ are T cell markers, this panel does not stain macrophages. Temporal arteritis

answer_options.explanation: CD3 / CD68. The hallmark of temporal arteritis is a transmural lymphocytic (T cells) and macrophagic infiltrate, which can be demonstrated with antibodies against CD3 and CD68, respectively. B cells (CD20+) and plasma cells (CD138+) are not diagnostic findings. the combination of CD34 and CD68 highlights histiocytes, myofibroblasts and small blood vessels but excludes T cells. although CD4+ and CD8+ are T cell markers, this panel does not stain macrophages. Temporal arteritis

title: Question 6
stem: A patient is diagnosed with temporal arteritis. What key finding is demonstrated with the special stain used in the image above?
teaching_point: Loss of elastic fibers. The Verhoeff-Van Gieson stain is a special stain used to demonstrate elastic fibers. In the setting of temporal arteritis, it can be used to evaluate areas of loss and disorganization of elastic fibers. intimal hyperplasia is visible with H&E and does not require special stains. tunica media fibrosis is a nonspecific finding and can be observed in the scenario of healed arteritis. myxoid degeneration is not a characteristic finding of temporal arteritis. Temporal arteritis

title: Question 7
stem: A squamous cell carcinoma of the eyelid is excised with negative margins. The tumor size in greatest dimension measures 12 mm and the tumor does not invade the tarsal plate or eyelid margin. What is the appropriate primary tumor (T) stage?
teaching_point: . T2a. Squamous cell carcinoma of the eyelid measuring > 10 mm but ≤ 20 mm is a T2. More specifically, this tumor would be categorized as a T2a due to the lack of invasion to the tarsal plate of the eyelid. stage Tis represents an in situ lesion. T1 tumors are ≤ 10 mm in greatest dimension. T3 tumors are > 20 mm but ≤ 30 mm in greatest dimension. T4 tumors invade adjacent ocular, orbital or facial structures. Staging-eyelid

title: Question 8
stem: Which entity would be assessed using the eyelid carcinoma staging criteria as defined by the AJCC (8th edition)?
teaching_point: . Infiltrative basal cell carcinoma. Carcinomas of the eyelid, including basal cell carcinoma, are staged using the eyelid carcinoma staging system (AJCC 8th edition). cutaneous carcinoma of the head and neck extending to the eyelid is staged with the cutaneous carcinoma of the head and neck classification system. diffuse large B cell lymphoma (DLBCL) is not considered carcinoma. If DLBCL arose from the eye adnexa and extended into the eyelid, it would be staged using the AJCC Ocular Adnexal Lymphoma staging protocol. Merkel cell carcinoma is staged using the AJCC Skin Merkel Cell Carcinoma staging protocol. melanoma is not classified as carcinoma and is staged based on whether it arises from the conjunctiva (using the AJCC Conjunctival Melanoma staging protocol) or from the eyelid skin itself (using the AJCC Melanoma of Skin staging protocol). Staging-eyelid

title: Question 9
stem: A palpebral melanoma invades the substantia propria and measures < 2 mm in thickness (see image above). What is the appropriate pT stage?
teaching_point: . pT2a. The melanoma arises in nonbulbar (palpebral) conjunctiva and invades the substantia propria but to a thickness of < 2 mm, corresponding to a pT2a classification. pTis is used for in situ disease. pT1 designations are used for melanomas of the bulbar conjunctiva that do not qualify for higher stages. pT3a refers to conjunctival melanomas of any size that invade the globe. pT4 refers to conjunctival melanomas of any size that invade the central nervous system. Staging-conjunctiva melanoma

title: Question 10
stem: Which of the following lesions should be staged using the conjunctival melanoma staging protocol (AJCC 8th edition)?
teaching_point: . Melanoma arising in the anterior, external portion of the eyeball. The anterior portion of the eyeball is covered by the bulbar conjunctiva; therefore, the neoplasm would be staged using the conjunctival melanoma staging protocol (AJCC 8th edition). a melanoma arising from the uvea would be staged according to the uveal melanoma staging protocol. a cutaneous melanoma would be staged according to the melanoma of the skin protocol. Answers D and E are incorrect because sebaceous carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma of the conjunctiva would be staged using the conjunctiva carcinoma staging protocol. Staging-conjunctiva melanoma

title: Question 11
stem: A squamous cell carcinoma of the conjunctiva is excised with negative margins and the carcinoma is negative for invasion beyond the conjunctival basement membrane. What is the appropriate primary tumor (T) stage?
teaching_point: Tis. Squamous cell carcinoma in situ of the conjunctiva does not invade through the conjunctival basement membrane and is designated as Tis by the AJCC (8th edition). T1 tumors are ≤ 5 mm in greatest dimension and invade beyond the basement membrane without involvement of adjacent structures. T2 tumors are > 5 mm in greatest dimension and invade beyond the basement membrane without involvement of adjacent structures. T3 tumors involve adjacent structures (excluding the orbit). T4 tumors invade the orbital soft tissue without bone invasion. Staging-conjunctiva carcinoma

T4 tumors invade the orbital soft tissue without bone invasion. Staging-conjunctiva carcinoma

title: Question 12
stem: Which entity would be assessed using the conjunctival carcinoma staging criteria as defined by the AJCC (8th edition)?
teaching_point: Sebaceous carcinoma. Carcinomas of the conjunctiva, including sebaceous carcinoma, are staged using the conjunctival carcinoma staging system (AJCC 8th edition). staging is not needed for mild conjunctival squamous intraepithelial neoplasia / dysplasia, which is confined to the lower third of the epithelium, as it does not reach the threshold for squamous cell carcinoma in situ, defined as full thickness epithelial dysplasia. Answers A, B and D are incorrect because diffuse large B cell lymphoma, melanoma and plasmacytoma are not carcinomas and are not staged using this system. Staging-conjunctiva carcinoma

stem: Retinoblastomas can occur both in sporadic and familial forms. Which of the following features is more indicative of a sporadic tumor?
teaching_point: . Late age of onset. Familial tumors result from germline mutations in the Rb gene and are more likely to be bilateral or trilateral. title: Question 16
stem: A patient with sudden, painless vision loss is diagnosed with central retinal artery occlusion. What layer of the retina would be expected to be affected?
teaching_point: Ganglion cell layer. The central retinal artery supplies the inner layers of the retina, including much of the inner nuclear layer, the inner plexiform layer, the ganglion cell layer and the nerve fiber layer. Anatomy & histology-retina

title: Question 17
stem: Which finding in the sample seen above is characteristic of pterygium?
teaching_point: . Elastosis of the lamina propria with corneal invasion. Elastosis is the key morphological finding in pterygia. The infiltration of the corneal stroma is important to be able to differentiate it from a pinguecula when there is no clinical information. Epithelial atrophy with goblet cell loss is nonspecific and can be seen in dry eye syndrome or superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis. Neovascularization is present in most of pterygia but is also seen in many other conjunctival inflammatory processes. Fragmentation of the Bowman layer may be present but is also seen in keratoconus and other chronic keratitis. Pterygium

title: Question 18
stem: Which of the following is the most important risk factor of pterygium?
teaching_point: . Sun exposure is the most important risk factor. Human papillomavirus is not strongly associated with either pterygium or conjunctival intraepithelial neoplasia. Chlamydia psittaci has been associated with some MALT lymphomas but has not been associated with pterygium. Alcohol and tobacco have been listed as possible predisposing factors but are not as prevalent as sun exposure. Pterygium

title: Question 21
stem: A 68 year old man presents with a skin colored nodule of the lateral left lower eyelid. Based on the histologic image, what is the most likely etiology of the lesion?
teaching_point: . Blockage of an eccrine sweat gland. The histologic image shows an epithelial lining composed of a dual layer of cuboidal cells, consistent with the diagnosis of eccrine hidrocystoma. Eccrine hidrocystomas are caused by blockages of eccrine sweat glands. Apocrine hidrocystomas result from blockages of apocrine sweat glands (A). Entrapped embryonal tissue is encountered in a dermoid cyst (C) and obstruction of a pilosebaceous unit may result in an epidermal cyst (D). Eyelid cysts

answer_options.text: Blockage of an apocrine sweat gland

title: Question 22
stem: A 1 year old girl undergoes excision of a superotemporal periocular nodule and the histologic findings are provided in the associated image. What is the diagnosis?
teaching_point: . Dermoid cyst. Periocular dermoid cysts most often occur in pediatric patients and are diagnosed histologically by the presence of adnexal structures in association with a bland stratified squamous epithelial cyst lining. Apocrine hidrocystomas demonstrate a cyst lining with an inner layer of elongated, eosinophilic cells with apical snouts and decapitation secretions (A). Eccrine hidrocystomas feature a cyst lining composed of a dual layer of cuboidal cells (C). Epidermal cysts have a cyst lining of bland keratinizing stratified squamous epithelium with a granular layer in the absence of adnexal structures (D). Eyelid cysts

title: Question 23
stem: The tumor above was found in the eye of a pediatric patient. What is the diagnosis?
teaching_point: . Dermolipoma. The tumor shows a stratified squamous epithelium, with skin appendages and a striking amount of adipose tissue. Answer C is incorrect due to the large amount of adipose tissue present in this lesion. there is no ectopic tissue from a second germ layer (i.e., cartilage, muscle). the lesion contains stratified squamous epithelium with skin appendages. there are no signs of elastotic degeneration and the presence of skin adnexa is not compatible with this diagnosis. Epibulbar choristoma

title: Question 24
stem: A 25 year old man with orthokeratology (specially fitted lenses) presents with severe pain in his left eye. The slit lamp examination shows a pattern of radial perineural and ring infiltrate in cornea. Corneal biopsy following negative direct smear examination on corneal scrapes shows focal ulceration of the surface with loss of epithelium; infiltrate of inflammatory cells, mainly polymorphonuclear cells and macrophages, are shown in the image above. What is the most likely diagnosis?
teaching_point: . Acanthamoeba keratitis. H&E sections at high power show several Acanthamoeba infiltrating corneal stroma in varying states of degeneration. Microscopic features on corneal biopsy also include ulceration, necrosis, neutrophilic and macrophage infiltrates; may show nonnecrotizing granulomatous inflammation. Encysted forms may be highlighted with PAS or GMS stains. a corneal biopsy for herpetic keratitis shows inflammatory infiltrates of mainly lymphocytes, plasma cells with stromal vascularization on H&E sections. Multinucleated giant cells around Descemet membrane are also identified. a corneal biopsy for mycotic (fungal) keratitis shows granulomatous, chronic nongranulomatous or rarely purulent inflammation on H&E sections; PAS or GMS positive for yeast or hyphal forms. microscopic findings of infiltration of the stroma by polymorphonuclear leukocytes occur in acute bacterial keratitis. The endothelium is often damaged and may have associated hypopyon and corneal perforation. Gram stain often shows bacteria in the stroma bordering the inflammatory infiltrate. Acanthamoeba keratitis

The endothelium is often damaged and may have associated hypopyon and corneal perforation. Gram stain often shows bacteria in the stroma bordering the inflammatory infiltrate. Acanthamoeba keratitis

title: Question 25
stem: A 52 year old man who is a soft contact lens wearer presents with left eye pain and vision changes. Ophthalmic exam shows a ring shaped corneal stromal defect. Corneal biopsy reveals necrosis, neutrophilic infiltrates as shown in the image above. Which of the following is the best choice of stain to confirm the diagnosis?
teaching_point: . GMS. GMS staining will highlight the encysted forms of amoeba in this description of Acanthamoeba keratitis. AFB Fite is used for Nocardia and would not stain amoebic forms. AFB Ziehl-Neelsen identifies acid fast organisms such as tuberculosis but would not stain amoebic forms. Gram stain is typically for bacterial organisms and would not be helpful in amoebic keratitis. mucicarmine is a stain for mucin and highlights cryptococcal forms, for example but is not helpful in this case of Acanthamoeba keratitis. Acanthamoeba keratitis

title: Question 26
stem: A 66 year old Caucasian male construction worker presents with the lesion shown above. Biopsy reveals the following histopathology. What is the likely diagnosis?
teaching_point: . Conjunctival intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3. The patient is an older, light skinned man, likely with significant UV exposure, putting him at higher risk for the conditions listed in the choices. The clinical image shows a gelatinous conjunctival lesion with feeder vessels at the limbus in the interpalpebral zone. The biopsy shows thickened conjunctival epithelium with loss of the normal maturation sequence. There is a proliferation of dysplastic squamous cells with high N/C ratios, mitotic activity, hyperchromatic and pleomorphic nuclei involving > two - thirds of the epithelial thickness (conjunctival intraepithelial neoplasia 3 / severe dysplasia). The basement membrane is intact. Primary acquired melanosis (choice B) is a proliferation of intraepithelial melanocytes that typically grow in a nested pattern. Pterygium (choice C) would present clinically as a wing-like, fleshy conjunctival lesion. Histopathology would show thin epithelium without dysplasia and underlying stromal fibrovascular tissue with solar elastosis. Conjunctival intraepithelial neoplasia is a precursor of squamous cell carcinoma (choice D). In squamous cell carcinoma, dysplastic cells invade beyond the basement membrane. Conjunctival intraepithelial neoplasia

title: Question 27
stem: Which of the following is a feature of conjunctival intraepithelial neoplasia?
teaching_point: . Proliferation of atypical squamous cells, which may be a precursor to squamous cell carcinoma. Conjunctival intraepithelial neoplasia is characterized by a proliferation of neoplastic squamous cells showing cellular atypia. It is considered a premalignant lesion that may progress to invasive squamous cell carcinoma. HPV 6 and 11 (choice A) are associated with squamous papilloma. HPV 16 and 18 are associated with conjunctival intraepithelial neoplasia. Goblet cell containing epithelium without dysplasia (choice B) is seen in other entities that may mimic conjunctival intraepithelial neoplasia, such as pterygium, pingueculae and squamous papilloma. CK7 (choice C) is expressed in normal conjunctiva and squamous papillomas but usually negative in dysplasia. PAS is positive in normal conjunctiva and squamous papillomas due to presence of goblet cells; however, goblet cells are usually lacking in conjunctival intraepithelial neoplasia. Conjunctival intraepithelial neoplasia

title: Question 28
stem: What do the intraepithelial clear spaces in this image represent?
teaching_point: Goblet cells are classically observed in these cases. Sometimes they are very numerous and they are characteristically slightly basophilic. Pagetoid spread of sebaceous cells is represented by malignant cells with atypical nuclei and optically empty vacuolated cytoplasm. Amelanotic spread of melanoma cells must show malignant features of the nuclei and is usually centrally located. An artifact will not be structured as observed in the cells here. Squamous papilloma-conjunctiva

title: Question 29
stem: Squamous papilloma is more frequently associated with
teaching_point: The most frequent associations are with HPV 6 and 11. Sun exposure is an important association in limbal papillomas but is less frequent than HPV. title: Question 30
stem: Based on the histologic features shown above, from what location is the biopsy taken?
teaching_point: Palpebral conjunctiva. The presence of dense fibroconnective tissue constituting the tarsal plate and sebaceous (Meibomian) glands is helpful for identifying the palpebral (tarsal) conjunctiva. the caruncle is identified by the presence of adnexal structures, such as pilosebaceous units. the cornea features corneal epithelium, Bowman membrane, corneal stroma, Descemet membrane and endothelium. Answers A and D are incorrect because the bulbar and forniceal conjunctivas are not seen in association with the tarsal plate. Anatomy & histology-conjunctiva

title: Question 32
stem: What is the key finding for chalazion seen in this photo?
teaching_point: Lipogranulomas. This is the key morphological finding in chalazion. The granulomas are usually nonnecrotizing. All the other changes such as lymphoplasmacytic chronic inflammation of the tarsal plate, exuberant granulation tissue and fibrosing chronic inflammation could be present but are nonspecific. title: Question 33
stem: What is the most common location of a chalazion?
teaching_point: Upper eyelid. The most common site of a chalazion is the upper eyelid, followed by the lower eyelid. Canthal location is rare. Chalazion of eyelid
